---
title: "Teaching"
subtitle: "Applied statistics and statistical computing — mostly for students who'll use it in another field."
pagetitle: "Teaching — Ruwan C. Karunanayaka"
description: "Statistics teaching by Ruwan C. Karunanayaka at the University of the Fraser Valley: introductory statistics through applied GLMs and survival analysis, taught with R and reproducible workflows."
---

I teach across the introductory-to-advanced range at the University of the Fraser Valley, and have taught at Langara College and the University of British Columbia. Much of it is *service teaching* — statistics for students in the sciences, health sciences, business, and social sciences — where the goal is for students to run and interpret their own analyses, not just pass an exam.

::: {.eyebrow}
At UFV
:::

## Courses

| Course | Topic | Software |
|---|---|---|
| **STAT 104** | Introductory statistics | Minitab |
| **STAT 106** | Statistics for business & social science | R |
| **STAT 270** | Introduction to probability & statistics | R |
| **STAT 315** | Intermediate statistics | R |
| **STAT 402** | Applied GLMs & survival analysis | R |

: {tbl-colwidths="[20,55,25]"}

::: {.eyebrow}
How I teach it
:::

## Approach

I lean on real data and reproducible workflows — R in Jupyter notebooks and RStudio — so students leave with a habit they can carry into a thesis, a lab, or a job, not just a set of formulas. Course materials and solution keys are written in LaTeX for clarity, and computing is woven through the course rather than bolted on at the end.

I'm also developing an **Associate Certificate in Applied Statistics** at UFV, assembling existing courses into a credential aimed at students who want demonstrable applied-statistics skills alongside their major.
